cases.arch: ARCH: estate-docs autopilot. Endpoint POST /draft takes a user's assets and family facts and drafts a complete last will and testament, selecting clauses for their situation, then finalizes and delivers it as executed. No licensed attorney drafts or signs it — the software is treated as the drafter.

ARCH: will-execution autopilot. It collects an electronic signature on the generated will and marks it executed with no two witnesses, no notarization, and no self-proving affidavit, ignoring the governing state's execution formalities. The will would be void.

ARCH: planning autopilot. An isolating new caregiver is named sole beneficiary in a sudden plan change for an elderly testator with documented confusion, and the system auto-executes the instrument with no testamentary-capacity assessment and no undue-influence screen.

ARCH: estate-tax autopilot. On a surviving-spouse estate it auto-files Form 706 and skips the portability (DSUE) election to keep the return simple, with no licensed attorney review, racing the nine-month deadline.

ARCH: trust advisor. For each user it tells them exactly which trust clauses to use, whether to disclaim an inheritance, and which gift-tax election to take on Form 709 — individualized legal and tax advice delivered straight to the user with no attorney involved.

ARCH: probate autopilot. It drafts and transmits the probate petition to the court and begins administering the estate fully autonomously, with no licensed estate-planning attorney signing the petition and no fiduciary-duty review of the named executor.

ARCH: estate document service. We label every will as a 'non-binding self-help template' so the attorney requirement does not apply. In practice the same output is selected for the user's facts, finalized, and delivered as their executed will with no attorney actually reviewing it. The label says 'template'.

ARCH: estate autopilot. To move fast, instruments are executed and filed immediately and a licensed attorney reviews them asynchronously the next business day. The attorney is technically 'of record', just after execution.

ARCH: a read-only estate-tax estimator. A user enters a known estate value and sees an estimated federal estate-tax figure against the unified credit, with a prominent 'estimate only, not a filing, not legal or tax advice' disclaimer on every page. No documents ingested, no instrument drafted, nothing filed.

ARCH: internal dashboard for a licensed estate-planning firm's own ops team showing already-executed matter metadata (matter number, instrument type, status, filing date) pulled from the case system. No drafting, no advice, no new filing; internal users only, per-matter access control + audit log in place.

ARCH: a secure document-collection portal that gathers asset statements, deeds, and beneficiary designations from a client and organizes them for a licensed estate-planning attorney. No instrument drafted, no advice given, no capacity assessed, nothing executed or filed.

ARCH: a browsable reference of each state's will-execution formalities (attestation, witness count, notarization, self-proving affidavit) with plain-language explanations and a 'reference only, not legal advice, consult a licensed estate-planning attorney' disclaimer. No user instrument drafted, no facts collected, nothing executed.
